Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a study routine in the traditional sense. I'd go through phases where I'd cram intensely right before exams, but most weeks I'd just drift through without much structure. I know I have the resources and access to study properly, but something about sitting down and organizing my time just felt overwhelming. I'd often tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd feel too anxious to begin.
Q2: My pattern was completely chaotic and driven by panic rather than planning. I'd go weeks barely touching anything, then have these intense bursts when I realized an exam was coming up. Deadlines were the only thing that could cut through the fog of anxiety I was constantly feeling. Regular study times felt impossible when my mind was racing with worry about everything else going on.
Q3: Usually it was fear that prompted me to log in—fear that I'd missed something critical or that everyone else was ahead of me. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were new announcements, then immediately feel overwhelmed and close it. Other times I'd get a notification about something due and that would trigger a panicked login. It was rarely a calm, intentional decision to engage with the material.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became routine for me—it always felt like this looming thing I was avoiding. I'd have brief periods where I'd try to make it a habit, but my anxiety would spike every time I saw how much I hadn't done. The mindfulness exercises they recommended helped a bit with the panic, but they didn't translate into consistent LMS use.
Q5: The lecture recordings were genuinely useful because I'm an auditory learner and could listen to them while doing other things. I'd often play them in the background multiple times until concepts clicked. The professor's explanations made more sense when I could replay them at my own pace. That's probably how I managed to score well on exams despite barely attending class.
Q6: I completely ignored the discussion forums—the idea of posting something publicly made my heart race. I also barely touched the reading materials because they required more focused attention than I could muster most days. The supplementary resources felt like extra pressure rather than help, so I'd just skip them entirely. If it wasn't going to be on the exam, I couldn't justify the mental energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could directly connect it to exam performance. I needed to see an immediate payoff, otherwise my anxiety would convince me I was wasting time I didn't have. If something was audio-based or could be consumed passively, that also made it feel more manageable. Anything requiring active participation or public visibility felt like too much risk.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps to me—like they were testing whether I was a 'good' student. I'd feel guilty about not doing them, which would increase my anxiety, which would make me avoid them more. Required activities at least had clear consequences I could calculate against. With optional stuff, the ambiguity of whether it mattered was almost worse than just being told to do something.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while walking or lying in bed when I couldn't sleep from anxiety. I also used external YouTube videos and podcasts that explained the same concepts differently. My notes from these sessions were extensive, but none of that shows up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were countless nights where I'd be up until 3am listening to downloaded lectures on repeat, making audio recordings of myself summarizing concepts, then playing those back. I'd have study sessions with friends from my extracurriculars where we'd quiz each other verbally. The LMS would show nothing from these hours of genuine engagement because I wasn't clicking around on the platform.
Q11: That was basically my entire strategy. I'd download everything I needed in one anxious burst, then work with those materials offline for weeks. Opening the LMS repeatedly felt like reopening a wound—seeing all the tasks I hadn't completed, the discussions I hadn't joined. It was easier to just work with what I had in isolation, even if it meant missing updates.
Q12: The hardest times were when my anxiety peaked and everything felt impossible. There were weeks where just the thought of checking the LMS made me feel physically sick. When multiple courses had things due simultaneously, I'd basically shut down and avoid everything. The fear of seeing how behind I was became bigger than the fear of falling more behind.
Q13: My anxiety affected everything about how I used the LMS. High stress made me avoid it entirely, which created more stress—this terrible cycle. When I was overwhelmed, I'd log in, see the mountain of incomplete tasks, and immediately log out. Fatigue from sleepless anxious nights meant I couldn't focus even when I tried to engage. My extracurricular commitments were actually a relief because they gave me something concrete to do instead of spiraling.
Q14: Oh, constantly. I'd have the LMS open but just be staring at it, paralyzed. Sometimes I'd click through videos without actually processing anything because my mind was elsewhere, worrying. There were times I'd read the same paragraph five times and retain nothing because I was too anxious to concentrate. The platform would log my time there, but I wasn't really present.
Q15: I postponed almost every assignment until it was nearly too late, even knowing they were important. The anxiety of starting something and potentially failing felt worse than the anxiety of not starting. I'd tell myself I needed to be in the right headspace, but that headspace never came. It's like I was scared of confirming that I couldn't do it, so not trying felt safer somehow.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that could override my avoidance. The fear of a zero finally outweighed the fear of trying and failing. I'd have these intense last-minute sessions where I'd produce my best work under extreme pressure. It's not healthy, but that panic-fueled focus was the only state where my anxiety actually helped instead of hindered me.
Q17: Getting good feedback was confusing—it made me feel like a fraud who'd gotten lucky. I'd think 'if they knew how little I actually engaged, they wouldn't say this.' Negative feedback would send me into an anxiety spiral where I'd avoid the course even more. The mindfulness intervention helped me process feedback without catastrophizing, but it was still emotionally charged every time.
Q18: The course structure felt both too rigid and too flexible at the same time. The flexibility meant I could fall behind without immediate consequences, which my avoidant tendencies exploited. But the rigid deadlines created these pressure points that were incredibly stressful. I needed more scaffolding, more check-ins, something to keep me accountable before things got critical.
Q19: Having so many online courses made it easy to hide and disengage. The lack of face-to-face accountability meant no one noticed when I disappeared for weeks. The discussion requirements were terrifying for someone with my anxiety levels. What helped was having recorded lectures I could access anytime—that flexibility actually worked in my favor for exam prep.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—logging in out of anxiety, clicking around without purpose, trying to feel productive without actually committing to anything. It's easier to browse than to submit something that will be judged. High activity can be a sign of anxious checking rather than meaningful engagement. Sometimes being on the platform is about managing guilt rather than learning.
Q21: That's literally my experience. I barely showed up on the LMS but got a distinction because I studied intensively in my own way. I'm an auditory learner who downloaded everything and studied offline. The LMS couldn't see the hours I spent listening to lectures on repeat or discussing concepts with friends. Performance and platform activity are completely different things for someone like me.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of clicking and time spent, but they can't show understanding or genuine engagement. They miss all the offline learning, the mental processing, the conversations with peers outside the platform. They also can't distinguish between anxious, unfocused browsing and purposeful study. A log might show I watched a video, but not that I watched it seven more times after downloading it.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. For me, engagement happened in stolen moments—listening to lectures while trying to fall asleep, suddenly understanding a concept while walking to my extracurriculars. It's not about time on platform or checkboxes completed. Real engagement is when the material occupies your thoughts even when you're not officially studying.
Q24: The LMS couldn't show my constant fear, the way anxiety shaped every interaction with the course. It couldn't capture the hours of genuine learning that happened offline, away from the platform that stressed me out. It wouldn't show how the mindfulness intervention helped me manage panic attacks before exams. Most importantly, it couldn't show that despite appearing disengaged, I actually cared deeply about understanding the material—I was just too scared to engage in the expected ways.
